refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the acceptance criterion (≥0.999 availability), specifies the measurement scope (bidirectional link), and defines the evaluation period as contractually agreed. This ensures testability and traceability to stakeholder needs (Tele-operator, Site Manager, Safety Manager).
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during the evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up) / (total_evaluation_time). Log link state transitions with timestamps. Compare measured availability against the 0.999 threshold at evaluation period end.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry link from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The NGHE solution shall detect link failures and initiate recovery within defined time bounds.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The NGHE solution shall log link availability metrics for post-evaluation analysis.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant wireless communication channels (primary + backup) with automatic failover logic; monitor link health continuously and switch to backup upon primary failure detection.

DP-B: Deploy a hybrid communication architecture combining cellular (primary) and satellite (backup) links with intelligent routing and seamless handover; include network abstraction layer to mask link transitions from application layer.
DP-C: Establish a single high-reliability wired tether-based link with error correction and retransmission protocols; suitable only for tethered vehicles.

DP-D: Implement software-defined networking (SDN) overlay with multi-path routing, real-time link quality monitoring, and dynamic path selection to maximize availability across heterogeneous network substrates.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Deploy a hybrid communication architecture combining cellular (primary) and satellite (backup) links with intelligent routing and seamless handover; include network abstraction layer to mask link transitions from application layer.

potential_sources: DP-B couples teleoperation link availability to network infrastructure selection (cellular + satellite), which is decoupled from vehicle control logic (FR-NGHE-010.1/010.2 are independent of how the link is realized).
DP-B introduces dependency on third-party network providers (cellular, satellite), which is a business/operational risk but not a functional coupling within NGHE design.
Handover logic (seamless transition) couples to application-layer telemetry/tasking protocols; mitigation: network abstraction layer isolates this coupling.
coupling_check.notes: DP-B is preferred over DP-A (single redundancy insufficient for 0.999 target in mobile environments) and DP-C (tether unsuitable for heavy equipment mobility). DP-D (SDN) is deferred as a future enhancement. Hybrid cellular+satellite provides geographic coverage and provider diversity, supporting 0.999 availability in varied terrain.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Cellular and satellite network providers maintain ≥0.99 availability independently; combined availability via failover can approach 0.999.
ASSUMPTION: Handover latency (cellular↔satellite) is acceptable to tele-operator (typically <500ms); if not, DP-B must be revised.
RISK: Satellite link latency (>500ms round-trip) may degrade tele-operator experience; mitigation: predictive handover and buffering strategies.
RISK: Cost of dual-link infrastructure (cellular + satellite) may exceed budget; mitigation: phased deployment (cellular primary, satellite added later).
RISK: Regulatory approval for satellite link in certain jurisdictions; mitigation: early engagement with regulators.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-B to explicitly specify: (1) failover detection threshold (e.g., 3 consecutive missed heartbeats = link failure), (2) handover latency budget (<500ms), (3) network abstraction layer API (e.g., standardized command/telemetry message format), and (4) fallback behavior if both links fail (e.g., safe stop of vehicle).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the hybrid communication architecture (cellular + satellite), link health monitoring, failover logic, network abstraction layer, and handover coordination. It interfaces with the Vehicle module (via Tasking and Telemetry) and Safety Services (via SafetyAlert for link failure notifications).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Commands from tele-operator (via Teleoperation module) to Vehicle; encapsulated in network-agnostic format by network abstraction layer.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle state and sensor data (via Teleoperation module) to tele-operator; received from Vehicle and forwarded to tele-operator interface.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Link failure or degradation alerts from Teleoperation module to Safety Services; triggers safe-stop or operator notification.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstraction of underlying cellular and satellite network resources; Teleoperation module manages link selection, failover, and health monitoring.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic, failover, and network abstraction. High containment: all hybrid communication, handover, and link monitoring logic resides here. Low diffusion: only external interfaces are Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Network Link (all well-defined).

Vehicle module should not own link availability; it owns vehicle control and sensor logic. Assigning DP-B to Vehicle would scatter network abstraction, failover, and handover logic across vehicle firmware, creating high diffusion and tight coupling to network infrastructure.

Safety Services owns safety policies and alerts (e.g., safe-stop on link failure) but should not own the link availability mechanism itself. Assigning DP-B to Safety Services would conflate link management with safety logic, reducing clarity and testability.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container; assigning DP-B here would leave implementation responsibility ambiguous. Link availability is a subsystem concern, not a system-level integration point.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-B (hybrid cellular+satellite architecture with failover). It naturally encapsulates network abstraction, link health monitoring, and handover logic. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal, reducing diffusion risk.
containment_risks: RISK: If Teleoperation module does not have direct access to network hardware abstraction (e.g., modem drivers, satellite transceiver APIs), implementation may require tight coupling to Vehicle or external network services. MITIGATION: Define a clear Network Link interface that abstracts hardware details.

RISK: Failover logic may require real-time decision-making (sub-100ms); if Teleoperation module runs on a separate processor/container with high latency to network hardware, failover may be delayed. MITIGATION: Co-locate failover logic with network hardware or use hardware-based failover (e.g., modem-level switching).

RISK: Teleoperation module may become a bottleneck if it serializes all command/telemetry traffic through a single failover decision point. MITIGATION: Use parallel processing or hardware-based load balancing for high-throughput scenarios.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the Network Link interface specification: (1) define the API for link selection, health monitoring, and failover triggers; (2) specify whether failover is hardware-based (modem-level) or software-based (Teleoperation module); (3) document latency budgets for failover detection and handover completion; (4) define the contract for SafetyAlert notifications (e.g., link failure vs. degradation thresholds).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-B (hybrid cellular+satellite with failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 link availability). The DP specifies a concrete solution approach (redundant links, intelligent routing, seamless handover) that is traceable to the FR. Assumptions about provider availability and handover latency are documented and can be validated during design refinement.

diffusion_risks: Failover logic may diffuse across Teleoperation module and Vehicle module if vehicle firmware must participate in link selection. MITIGATION: Enforce strict separation via Network Link interface; Vehicle module treats link as opaque.

Handover coordination may require synchronization with Safety Services (e.g., pause vehicle motion during handover). MITIGATION: Define SafetyAlert protocol to signal link transitions; Safety Services reacts asynchronously.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves high containment (0.95) for DP-B. All hybrid communication, failover, and handover logic is localized. External interfaces are minimal and well-defined (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link). No significant diffusion risk if Network Link interface is properly abstracted.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: FR-NGHE-010 is verifiable via continuous link state monitoring and availability calculation. Teleoperation module can log link transitions and compute availability metrics. Test plan: simulate link failures (cellular outage, satellite latency spike) and verify failover occurs within latency budget and availability target is met over evaluation period.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Teleoperation module is the clear owner of link availability. Responsibilities are well-allocated: Teleoperation owns hybrid architecture and failover; Vehicle owns vehicle control; Safety Services owns safe-stop policy. No ambiguity in ownership or interface contracts.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-B to specify failover detection algorithm (e.g., heartbeat-based, signal-strength-based) and latency budget (<500ms).
Define Network Link interface specification (API, message format, failover triggers) to ensure Teleoperation module can abstract network hardware.
Document SafetyAlert protocol: what events trigger alerts (link failure, degradation, handover in progress) and how Safety Services reacts.
Establish acceptance criteria for link availability measurement: sampling interval, evaluation period duration, and statistical confidence level.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (command link, telemetry link, failure detection/recovery, metrics logging). DP-B is sound and well-allocated to Teleoperation module, but further refinement is needed at the next level to address sub-FRs and validate assumptions (provider availability, handover latency, failover detection algorithm). Proceeding to decompose sub-FRs will clarify implementation details and reduce risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from tele-operator to vehicle with latency <500ms.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry link from vehicle to tele-operator with latency <500ms.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The NGHE solution shall detect link failures within 3 seconds and initiate failover to backup link within 500ms.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The NGHE solution shall log link availability metrics (up/down state, transition timestamps, provider identity) for post-evaluation analysis and reporting.

assumptions: Cellular network provider maintains ≥0.99 availability in operational areas.
Satellite network provider maintains ≥0.99 availability as backup.
Combined availability via failover can achieve ≥0.999 target (assumes independent failures).
Handover latency (cellular↔satellite) is acceptable to tele-operator (<500ms).
Network abstraction layer can mask link transitions from application layer without loss of command/telemetry data.
Failover detection can be achieved via heartbeat or signal-strength monitoring with <3 second detection latency.
Safety Services can react to SafetyAlert notifications and initiate safe-stop within acceptable time bounds.
open_risks: Satellite link latency (>500ms round-trip) may degrade tele-operator experience; requires user acceptance testing.
Cost of dual-link infrastructure may exceed budget; requires business case review.
Regulatory approval for satellite link in certain jurisdictions; requires early engagement with regulators.
Failover detection algorithm (heartbeat vs. signal-strength) not yet selected; impacts detection latency and false-positive rate.
Handover coordination with Safety Services not yet detailed; risk of unsafe state transitions during failover.
evidence_needed: Network provider availability data (cellular and satellite) for operational areas.
Handover latency measurements from cellular↔satellite transition in field conditions.
Failover detection algorithm performance (detection latency, false-positive rate) via simulation or prototype testing.
Tele-operator acceptance criteria for link latency and handover experience.
Safety Services requirements for link failure notification and safe-stop response time.
Regulatory guidance on satellite link use in heavy equipment teleoperation.
Cost estimates for dual-link infrastructure (cellular modem + satellite transceiver + network services).
